fuzzy_prd:
We need a small toolkit of composable primitives for a secrets-management command-line tool. The primitives cover six distinct concerns. First, parse an octal file-permission string as typed on a command line into a concrete permission — accept a leading zero or not, reject strings shorter than three characters, and report both the canonical four-digit octal form and the symbolic rwx representation. Second, ensure output text always ends with exactly one trailing newline so it plays nicely with shell pipelines. Third, validate environment-variable names against two rulesets: a portable-charset ruleset (which bans null and equals sign) and a stricter POSIX shell ruleset (letters, digits, underscores only, no leading digit). Fourth, format a calendar instant as a machine-readable timestamp given the date-time components and a named timezone — zones with no UTC offset should show 'Z', positive-offset zones should show '+HH:MM'. Fifth, expand dollar-brace placeholders inside a template string by substituting supplied values — trim spaces from key lookups, fail clearly if a key is missing or if an opening delimiter is never closed. Sixth, redact secret values from a stream of output as it is written, even when a secret spans multiple writes — handle it the same way the masker module handles overlapping secrets in other command outputs: longer secrets take precedence over shorter ones that share a prefix.

One small follow-up from the questions that came in: for the file mode piece, a mode string must contain at least three characters. Anything shorter, including empty string, should be rejected with exactly 'error=invalid_filemode
mode=<value>
'. On success it should return exactly 'octal=<4-digit-octal>
perm=<10-char-symbolic>
'. The symbolic form uses '-' as the leading type character for a regular permission set, so that leading character is part of the 10-char output.

Also, for placeholder expansion, if a referenced placeholder key has no value in the replacements map, return exactly 'error=key_not_found
key=<missing_key>
'. If a placeholder is opened but never closed, return exactly 'error=tag_not_closed
delimiter=}
'. When expansion works, emit the expanded text verbatim with no trailing newline.

And for BOM detection, please use these exact mappings: UTF-8 BOM is EF BB BF -> encoding=utf-8. UTF-16LE: FF FE -> utf-16le. UTF-16BE: FE FF -> utf-16be. UTF-32LE: FF FE 00 00 -> utf-32le. UTF-32BE: 00 00 FE FF -> utf-32be. UTF-32 signatures take precedence over UTF-16 when they share a prefix. No known BOM -> encoding=none.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: func Parse(mode string) (FileMode, error); func AddNewLine(b []byte) []byte; func IsEnvarName(name string) bool; func IsEnvarNamePosix(name string) bool

trigger_keywords: what is the timestamp format for UTC timezone
what suffix is used for zero-offset zones
how is the Europe/Amsterdam timezone rendered
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: UTC timezone: trailing 'Z' suffix (e.g. '2018-01-01T01:01:01Z'). Positive-offset timezone: '+HH:MM' suffix (e.g. '2018-01-01T01:01:01+01:00'). The format is YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SS<offset>. The timestamps flag must be true for formatting; if false, a different (human-readable) format is used.

trigger_keywords: what happens when a secret partially matches at end of stream
are incomplete matches at stream end emitted
what is emitted for bytes that only partially match a secret
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Bytes that form only a partial match of a secret at the very end of the stream — never completed — are emitted unmasked. Consecutive fully-matched secrets collapse into a single replacement (one replacement per matched secret, not one for the entire run). The mask_string replaces each matched secret occurrence.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: handle it the same way the masker module handles overlapping secrets in other command outputs: longer secrets take precedence over shorter ones that share a prefix
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The masker package in internals/cli/masker/ implements streaming replacement. When one mask is a prefix of another, the longer mask is matched first. The masker buffers incoming bytes and emits them once it is certain no pending match can extend further.

oracle_data.fallback_response: Please refer to the start.md specification. Actions: parse_filemode, add_newline, validate_envar_name, validate_posix_name, format_time, inject_template, detect_encoding, lookup_encoding, mask.

feature1_filemode.json.description: Parse a textual octal file-permission string (as a command-line flag would supply) into a concrete permission set. A valid input is interpreted as a base-8 number and must contain at least three digits; a leading zero is optional. On success the parsed value is reported both as its canonical 4-digit octal form and as its symbolic rwx permission string. Inputs shorter than three characters are rejected as invalid.

feature2_posix_newline.json.description: Ensure a chunk of output text is terminated by exactly one trailing newline so it is POSIX-compliant. If the input already ends with a newline it is returned unchanged; otherwise a single newline byte is appended. Empty input yields just a newline. The raw resulting bytes are emitted verbatim.

feature3_1_envar_charset.json.description: Validate whether a string is an acceptable environment-variable name under the portable character set (IEEE Std 1003.1): every character must fall within the allowed portable ranges, and the NUL and '=' characters are forbidden. An empty string is invalid. A valid name reports success; an invalid name reports failure together with a neutral error category.

feature3_2_envar_posix.json.description: Validate whether a string is a POSIX-shell-compliant environment-variable name: it may contain only ASCII letters, digits, and underscores, must be non-empty, and must not begin with a digit. Letters of any case are allowed. Names that are empty, start with a digit, or contain any other character are rejected.

feature4_timestamp.json.description: Format a calendar instant as a machine-readable timestamp. Given the date/time components and a named time zone, with timestamp mode enabled, the instant is rendered in the standard internet date-time format (date, time, and the UTC offset of the zone). A zone with no offset is shown with a 'Z' suffix; a positive-offset zone is shown with the explicit '+hh:mm' offset.

feature5_template_inject.json.description: Inject named values into a template string. Placeholders are written between the start delimiter '${' and the end delimiter '}', and the trimmed text between them is the lookup key; each placeholder is replaced by the value supplied for that key, while text outside placeholders is copied verbatim. Parsing is left-to-right and treats a second '${' before the matching '}' as part of the current key. A template with no placeholders is returned unchanged. If a referenced key has no supplied value, a neutral key-not-found error naming the missing key is reported; if a placeholder is opened but never closed, a neutral tag-not-closed error naming the missing delimiter is reported.

feature6_masking.json.description: Mask sensitive substrings as they stream through an output writer. The writer is configured with a list of secret byte sequences and a replacement string; any occurrence of a secret in the streamed bytes (even spanning multiple writes, even when one secret encloses another) is replaced by the replacement string, and consecutive masked bytes collapse into a single replacement. Bytes that only partially match a secret at the end of the stream are emitted unmasked. Input is supplied as an ordered list of write chunks; the fully processed output is emitted verbatim.

feature7_1_encoding_detect.json.description: Detect the character encoding of a byte stream from its leading byte-order mark (BOM). The leading bytes are checked against the known BOM signatures for the Unicode transformation formats, with the longer (UTF-32) signatures taking precedence over the shorter (UTF-16) ones that share a prefix. If a known BOM is found, the corresponding encoding is reported; otherwise the result is 'none'. Input bytes are given as a hex string.

feature7_2_encoding_lookup.json.description: Resolve a character-encoding name (case-insensitive) to a supported encoding. The Unicode transformation formats are recognized, including the UTF-32 variants and explicit little-endian/big-endian forms. A recognized name reports its canonical encoding label; an unrecognized name reports a neutral unsupported-encoding error naming the requested input.
